title: Chapter 6: Whitby's
content: *[AUDIO FILE: episode_zero_raw_take_01.wav]*
*[00:00:00 — Ambient room tone. The hum of a laptop fan. A microphone being adjusted, fingers fumbling against the mesh. A deep breath.]*
This is episode zero.

This isn't a polished production. There's no intro music, no sponsor read, no professionally mixed sound design that makes you feel like you're sliding into a story. This is me, at three in the morning, in my apartment that smells like cold coffee and bad decisions, trying to figure out what happened to my best friend.
Her name is Lucy Chen.

She's twenty-five years old. She has 1.2 million followers across three platforms, and every single one of them thinks she's on a wellness retreat in Sedona right now, posting pre-scheduled content about gratitude journals and crystal water and whatever else her management team queued up to keep the brand alive while she—
While she's gone.
She's been missing for fourteen days.
And I think something took her.
*[A pause. The creak of a chair. A whispered curse, barely caught by the mic.]*

I don't know what this is yet. I don't know if anyone will ever hear this. But I'm going to find out what happened to Lucy, and I'm going to start recording everything, because the one thing I've learned from three years of social media is this: if it's not documented, it didn't happen.
So. Here's the documentation.
*[00:02:17 — END OF FILE]*

Maya saved the file and closed her laptop. Her hands were shaking. Not from the cold, though her apartment was freezing — she'd turned the heat off three days ago to save money and kept forgetting to turn it back on. From the weight of what she'd just committed to.
She'd said it out loud. *Something took her.*
Not *Lucy went missing.* Not *Lucy disappeared.* She'd said *took.* Like there was a taker. Like there was a hand that reached out and pulled her best friend into the dark.

She sat in the silence of her apartment and listened to the refrigerator cycle. A siren threaded through the Mission outside, dopplering away toward General Hospital. The smell of her third attempt at pour-over coffee — bitter, over-extracted, abandoned hours ago — hung in the air like a question nobody wanted to answer.
She needed to get out of this apartment. She needed to think.

Whitby's was the kind of bar that didn't exist in the Mission anymore, or shouldn't have — a dark, wood-paneled holdout from the nineties, all nautical rope and brass portholes and a ship's wheel mounted behind the counter like a joke whose punchline had been forgotten. It smelled of old whiskey soaked into old wood, with a faint undercurrent of rain that had blown in every time the door opened. The kind of place that survived on regulars and stubbornness.

Chloe was already there when Maya walked in, sitting at the far end of the bar with her back to the wall, facing the entrance. Occupational habit. She had a glass of whiskey in front of her, neat, no ice, no small talk, just amber liquid catching the amber light. Her laptop bag was wedged between her feet. The screen of her phone glowed blue on the bartop.
Maya slid onto the stool next to her. "Hey."
"Hey." Chloe didn't look up from her phone. "Drink?"

The bartender — Diego, who'd been pouring Maya's drinks since she was twenty-two and forging a new identity every six months wasn't yet a survival skill — appeared with a cocktail glass already in hand. "Oat milk latte, Martini glass, because you're unhinged." He set it down with the ceremonial gravity of a man presenting a fine wine. Steam curled from the surface.
"You're a king," Maya said.

"I'm an enabler." He wiped his hands on his apron and moved away. The bar was half-full, a Tuesday night crowd of people who had nowhere better to be — low talk, the clink of glasses, someone's phone playing a baseball game on mute. Normal. Ordinary. Maya tried to let it settle her.

She wrapped her hands around the cold glass and let the warmth of the latte seep through. The oat milk was sweet and faintly floral, and she drank it like a person trying to remember what normal tasted like.
"We need to think like hunters," Maya said. "Not hackers."
Chloe finally looked up. "Hunters."
"We've been reacting. Lucy's watch sends a code, we decode it. You find an app, we dig into it. We're chasing breadcrumbs. We need to get ahead."

Chloe took a slow sip of her whiskey. The burn didn't even make her blink anymore. "Okay. Huntress mode." She pulled a folded sheaf of papers from her bag and smoothed them on the bartop — architectural blueprints, stamped with the San Francisco City Planning seal. "Aeterna's floor plans. Pulled from the city planning office public records. Their building permit application is public because of the IPO filing — they had to disclose structural modifications."

Maya leaned in. The blueprints showed a building silhouette, clean lines, annotations in architectural shorthand. "Seven stories above ground," Chloe traced with her finger. "Open-plan offices, conference rooms, a gym, a cafeteria. Standard tech campus bullshit. Three sublevels." Her finger moved lower. "Sublevel A: server storage. Documented, permitted, boring. Sublevel C — mechanical, HVAC, utility runs. Also boring."
Her finger stopped.
"Sublevel B."

Maya looked at the footprint. The floor plan for Sublevel B was a single massive rectangle, unbroken by interior walls, spanning the entire foundation of the building. No support columns annotated. No mechanical rooms. No documented purpose.

"This is the largest space in the entire building," Chloe said. "It spans the full footprint. Maya, that's — that's architecturally impossible for a server room. Server rooms need environmental controls, cable runs, access corridors, fire suppression. This has none of that on the permit. It's just one big empty box labeled 'cold storage' on the filing."
"Cold storage." Maya's mouth tasted like oat milk and something sour underneath. "For eight hundred and forty-seven women."
"Or for whatever's keeping them alive down there."
The word *alive* hung between them. Chloe drank. Maya held her glass without sipping.
"Tell me about Vlad," Maya said.

Chloe set down her whiskey. "Alexander Vlad. CEO and founder of Aeterna Health Technologies. Officially: born in Brașov, Romania, 1985. Emigrated to the US in 2003. Educated at the University of Bucharest, molecular biology. Founded Aeterna in 2011 with twelve million in seed funding from a consortium of offshore accounts."
"Unofficially?"

Chloe's jaw tightened. "Unofficially, there is no birth certificate on file with the Romanian government. I checked. There's a record of birth — a single line in a parish register from 1985 — but no state-issued certificate. The University of Bucharest has no record of a student named Alexander Vlad, or any variant spelling, in any year. No enrollment, no degree, no dissertation."
"Maybe he went by a different name."

"Maybe. Or maybe Alexander Vlad didn't exist before 2011, when he appeared in Delaware with twelve million dollars and a corporate charter." Chloe turned her phone toward Maya, showing a screen of banking documents — wire transfers, routing numbers, flags. "I traced the seed money through three shell companies before it hit a wall. The final holding company is domiciled in the Cayman Islands and funded by a Swiss bank account opened in 1922."
Maya stared at the number. 1922. "The bank was founded in—"

"1922. Yes. The same bank. The account that funded Aeterna's founding was opened the year the bank was established. Ninety years before Vlad supposedly incorporated." Chloe's voice was level, clinical, but her knuckles were white around her glass. "Every piece of his identity is a cover, Maya. The education, the birthplace, the immigration timeline — it's all fabricated. It's professional, it's thorough, and it held up to every background check Wall Street ran before the IPO. But it's not real."

Maya set down her latte. The glass was warm in her hand, then cold. The bar noise seemed to recede, leaving just the two of them and the weight of 1897 and the journal entries in Vlad's cloud — entries written in fountain pen, in Romanian and French and English, spanning a century of hunger.
"So he's either the most elaborate fraud in Silicon Valley," Maya said slowly, "or—"
"Or he's exactly what the journal entries suggest."
Neither of them said the word. They didn't have to. It was sitting on the bartop between them like a third drink neither of them ordered.
Maya broke the silence first. "I recorded something tonight. A podcast. Episode zero."
Chloe raised an eyebrow. "A podcast."
"Yeah."
"You want to fight a centuries-old biotech vampire with—" Chloe made a vague gesture, her whiskey sloshing against the glass, "—content creation?"
"When you say it like that, it sounds stupid."
"It sounds stupid because it is—"

"It's not about evidence for the police." Maya cut her off, and something in her voice made Chloe stop. "The police need proof. Chain of custody, forensic analysis, a DA willing to prosecute. We've got Morse code from a smartwatch, a blood-type filter in a wellness app, and a CEO whose documentation is fake. None of that gets us a warrant. None of that even gets us a meeting."
"So what does a podcast get us?"

"Public opinion." Maya leaned forward. "Vlad needs the IPO. He needs Wall Street to believe Aeterna is the next Apple, the next Impossible Foods, the next whatever. Clean, shiny, market-ready. He needs eight hundred and forty-seven women to be invisible until the stock vests and the money moves. A viral true crime podcast is the one thing his PR team can't spin. You can't issue a press release about a missing woman when a hundred thousand people are listening to her best friend ask what happened to her. You can't make the story go away when the algorithm picks it up and serves it to every true crime fan in America."

Chloe was quiet for a long moment. Her eyes moved — thinking, calculating, running models. The bar sounds filled the gap: the low thump of music from somewhere in the back, ice cracking in a shaker, laughter from a table near the window.
"You're going to weaponize the algorithm," Chloe said.
"I'm going to weaponize the algorithm."
"Jesus." Chloe took a long pull of her whiskey. "Lucy would love this. She'd say you're finally using your engagement metrics for evil."
"Good evil. Protective evil." Maya's throat tightened. She took a breath. "Lucy always said the only thing worse than being ignored is being silenced. I won't let them silence her."
Chloe nodded slowly. Then she stiffened.
The shift was immediate — Chloe's whole body went rigid, her hand moving almost imperceptibly off the bartop, hovering near her bag. Her eyes fixed on something past Maya's shoulder.
"What?" Maya said.
"Don't look now." Chloe's voice was barely a breath. "But we might be made."

Maya's pulse spiked. She kept her eyes on Chloe, fighting every instinct to turn. Her peripheral vision caught it anyway: a figure at the far end of the bar. Tall. Pale. Sitting without a drink, without a menu, without any of the small rituals that marked a person who'd come to a bar to be at a bar. And on his chest, hanging from a lanyard against a dark shirt — a badge. White and blue. The Aeterna logo, that clean geometric A, catching the amber light.

He took out his phone. Held it up, not quite naturally — the screen facing them, the brief blue glow of a camera app — and then put it away. Stood. Left through the front door without ordering a single thing.
The cold air that swept in with the door's swing carried the smell of rain and wet pavement.
"We need to go," Chloe said. "Now. Kitchen exit."

They moved. Maya grabbed her bag, Chloe swept the blueprints off the bar in one motion, and they slipped past the service counter through the swinging kitchen door — the heat and clatter of the line hitting them like a wall, a cook yelling something in Spanish, the blast of a dishwasher vent — and then out the back into a narrow alley that smelled of grease and wet concrete and fog coming in off the bay.
Rain was starting. Fine, cold, horizontal. It stuck to Maya's skin and beaded on Chloe's glasses.
Chloe pulled out her phone. Her face was lit by the screen's blue glow, and Maya watched her expression shift from alert to something worse — something like fear.
"What is it?" Maya said.

Chloe turned the phone toward her. A notification from her monitoring system — the one she'd built to watch Aeterna's internal employee badge database. Someone had accessed a security camera image from the Oakland Federal Building. The image showed a woman exiting the building. The woman was Chloe. Her real name — the one in the government database from the arrest at the tech protest two years ago, the one she'd thought was sealed — was attached to the access log.
"They know who I am," Chloe said.

Maya's stomach dropped. They started walking. Fast. Down the alley, right on 24th Street, past the taquerias and the bodegas and the murals glowing under streetlights in the thin rain. The Mission at night — usually alive, usually loud, but tonight the rain had driven people inside and the sidewalks were empty enough to feel exposed.
"How?" Maya's mind was sprinting ahead of her feet. "We were careful. We used encrypted channels, we met offline, we—"
"It doesn't matter." Chloe was walking fast, her bag bouncing against her hip. "They found me. They ran my face against—"

"Wait." Maya stopped. Chloe stopped two steps ahead, turning back. Rain on both their faces. "How did they find you at that bar? You've never been to Whitby's. I picked it twenty minutes before I texted you. There's no way they tracked you there through conventional surveillance unless they were already—"
She stopped. The pieces were rearranging themselves in her head. Not how did they find us at the bar. How did they find us at all.
"Chloe." Maya's voice was quiet now. Dangerous. "Did you ever download the Lumina app? Even to research it?"
Chloe's face changed. The fear was there, but underneath it — something worse. The look of a person realizing they'd walked into a trap they'd been proud of discovering.
"I created a burner account," Chloe said. "Two weeks ago."
"Did you allow health data access?"
"I—" Chloe swallowed. "I had to, to see the filtering interface. The blood-type catalog doesn't appear unless you grant health permissions. I used a throwaway email, a VPN, the whole—"

"The app has your phone's hardware ID. VPN doesn't mask that. The health data access gave it your biometrics — heart rate variability, sleep patterns, location data if you ever had location services on for anything else on that device." Maya was speaking fast now, the connections clicking like tumblers in a lock. "They didn't find you through hacking. They found you through their own front door. You walked in and handed them your digital fingerprint."
Chloe's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against her thighs, trying to stop it. The rain made the gesture useless. "They've been tracking me since the moment I signed up."

"They knew you were probing their systems before you found anything." The words came out cold, certain, because Maya could see the shape of it now — could see the whole architecture of the trap. "They *let* you find the public-facing stuff. The blood-type filter you accessed — that's the surface layer. The one they want curious people to discover. It's designed to make you think you've found the secret, so you stop digging."
"It's a honey trap," Chloe whispered. "The whole app. It's a honey trap. For the women they harvest—" Her voice cracked, "—and the people who try to expose them."

They stood on the corner of 24th and Mission. The BART station hummed underground beneath their feet. The rain was coming down harder now, and the streetlights made halos in the mist, and somewhere a car alarm went off and nobody came.
Chloe's hands were shaking. Not from the cold.
Maya grabbed her arm. Hard. Grounding.
"We need to go dark," Maya said. "Everything. Right now. Every account, every device, every digital footprint that connects either of us to Aeterna, to Lumina, to any of it. We burn it all tonight."
Chloe looked at her. Rain on her glasses. The fear was still there, but something else was rising beneath it — something that looked, against all reason, like anger.
"Okay," Chloe said. "Okay. Let's go."

They turned and walked into the rain, and the city closed around them, and somewhere in a building with seven stories above ground and three below, a system logged their departure from the bar and flagged it for review and appended it to a file that was already open.
